title: podman restore --keep sometimes fails
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

I want to be able to restore a container multiple times. As I understand it, in order to enable this I need to use the --keep argument with podman container restore. For one image, ubuntu:1604, this works. I can restore with --keep, attach, exit, and do it again. However with another container I've built, this fails. 

However if, instead of exiting the container, I use podman container stop, then restore --keep works.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**
lsw2/lisp is an image I have built.
```
podman container run -it --name foo lsw2/lisp
```
enters bash
run java application with interactive prompt
in another shell,
```
sudo podman container checkpoint --keep --file-locks --tcp-established foo
sudo podman container restore --keep --file-locks --tcp-established foo
podman container attach foo
```
quit the java application
now back at bash prompt 
```
exit
sudo podman container restore --keep --file-locks --tcp-established foo
```

**Describe the results you received:**
```

Error: crun: CRIU restoring failed -52.  Please check CRIU logfile /var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-containers/5d6ec6f7eaf52371daa88b2dd21c8f00ae8a452a2c7fa998c49df32a6cb67319/userdata/restore.log: OCI runtime error
```
The tail of restore.log says:

```
(00.040650)      9: Error (criu/files-reg.c:2143): Can't open file tmp/hsperfdata_lsw/9 on restore: No such file or directory
(00.040668)      9: Error (criu/files-reg.c:2086): Can't open file tmp/hsperfdata_lsw/9: No such file or directory
(00.040671)      9: Error (criu/mem.c:1349): `- Can't open vma
(00.041773) pie: 6: Task 9 exited, status= 1
(00.041914) pie: 2: Task 6 stopped with 19
(00.042751) mnt: Switching to new ns to clean ghosts
(00.042788) Unlink remap home/lsw/abcl-felix-cache/cache.lock.cr.1.ghost
(00.042873) Error (criu/cr-restore.c:2447): Restoring FAILED.
```

**Describe the results you expected:**

Able to attach and continue to interact with java program, same as if I stopped the container.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

If, instead of quitting the java application and exiting bash I do, from another shell
```sudo podman container stop foo```
the second restore succeeds.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
podman version 4.0.0-dev
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**


comments.body:
This sounds like a misunderstanding. `--keep` cannot be used the way you want to use it. Or better, it can work, but it can also fail.

The idea behind the `--keep` option is to keep log files and also the checkpoint data for error analysis. The way you are using it was not the intend.

To restore a checkpoint all files need to be the same as during checkpointing or else your restored process is accessing a file with changed content and that might lead to errors. Therefore CRIU checks at least the file size to have some confidence that the file did not change.

In your case it is a known thing with JVM and restore. The JVM has something called the `perfdata feature` which creates the files CRIU detects the file size changes. In your case you could tell the JVM to not creates those files using `-XX:-UsePerfData`.
That would probably fix the problem you are seeing, but you still use the restore feature in a way it is not supposed to be used and potentially unsafe.

What you want to do can only be done using the checkpoint `--export` feature. That would create a checkpoint archive which contains the actual checkpoint and the state of the filesystem during checkpointing. Only the combination of checkpoint and corresponding filesystem changes gives you a reliable restore.

Podman knows internally that the container has been checkpointed or not and probably should just refuse to do a restore for a container that is not in the `Checkpointed: true` state.

You can try to set `-XX:-UsePerfData` for the JVM in your container to see if that works for you, but you still would use potentially unsafe restores if other files are changing. Worse would be if the file changes, but not the file size. CRIU cannot detect this and your process is restored, but the content of the file is different than the restored application expects.

Thank you very much @adrianreber for the explanation! Yes, using -XX:-UsePerfData does let it work.  Repeating to make sure I understand: If I change any file after I resume a checkpoint, I won't be able to resume it again.

I had done some experiments with --export but the performance was too slow for what I'm aiming for. It took at least 10 seconds and sometimes low 20 seconds to import and restart a container, the longer times, IIRC. after I had done it a few times. I also saw the machine qcow2 file grow a lot more than it seemed it should have. That's anecdotal, I know. I will try to reproduce reliably.

Restoring from checkpoint using --keep is much much faster - 2 or 3 seconds - which is what I'm aiming for since I want to turn this into a command line tool for quick tasks (that happen to need a lot of state in order to be fast).

A few questions: 
1. Is the slow time for restore from export due to transferring the files to the host file system and, if so, can the exported checkpoint be saved within the podman machine image to reduce the time?
2. Along the lines of (1), can the checkpoint directories be cloned under a new id, and then resume on the new id, leaving leaving the original checkpoint untouched?
3. I haven't studied overlays so apologies if this makes no sense, but  I'm wondering whether I can, on resuming a checkpoint, add an overlay to capture any changes that happen once the checkpoint is resumed, which could be tossed before the next resume. 
4. Along the lines of (2), are there any places in the file system that can be written where the CRIU file checks won't be done, say in /tmp or in another volume?

> Thank you very much @adrianreber for the explanation! Yes, using -XX:-UsePerfData does let it work. Repeating to make sure I understand: If I change any file after I resume a checkpoint, I won't be able to resume it again.

Yes, if any file which is opened by any of the checkpointed processes changes in size, CRIU will not restore the process to avoid unnecessary possible failures. It could work but CRIU stays on the safe side.

> I had done some experiments with --export but the performance was too slow for what I'm aiming for. It took at least 10 seconds and sometimes low 20 seconds to import and restart a container, the longer times, IIRC. 

With export there is usually a compress/decompress (which can be disabled, by the way) and copying involved. Try to disable compression during checkpointing with `--compress none` (I think).

> A few questions:

One thing I need to mention, I am not familiar with MacOS and the whole podman setup on a Mac.

>     1. Is the slow time for restore from export due to transferring the files to the host file system and, if so, can the exported checkpoint be saved within the podman machine image to reduce the time?

Not sure how that works on a Mac.

>     2. Along the lines of (1), can the checkpoint directories be cloned under a new id, and then resume on the new id, leaving leaving the original checkpoint untouched?

You can manually create copies. That should work. You need the `checkpoint` directory and a couple of metadata files. It can be done.

>     3. I haven't studied overlays so apologies if this makes no sense, but  I'm wondering whether I can, on resuming a checkpoint, add an overlay to capture any changes that happen once the checkpoint is resumed, which could be tossed before the next resume.

Probably yes, but the checkpointing code does not really touch anything concerning the underlying file systems. In theory something like you suggested could be done.

>     4. Along the lines of (2), are there any places in the file system that can be written where the CRIU file checks won't be done, say in /tmp or in another volume?

If the changed files are only in directories mounted from the outside of the container, then it can work. Then you can ensure that the files have the correct content. Everything outside of the container which is usually bind mounted in the container is your responsibility, the checkpoint code tries to restore all files inside of the container as they were before.
@alanruttenberg If you want to skip the copy to your host os from the VM you have to use `podman machine ssh podman ...`

@Luap99 Thanks. I just tried that but get an error on restore. The last bit of the log is below. I presume that since I exported within podman machine I needed to import it in same. The commands, within a shell started with podman machine ssh```. Note that I verified that the export/import I did on my host does succeed. FWIW I have updated to 4.02. between my last comment and this one.

```sh
podman container checkpoint --file-locks --tcp-established --compress none a89615250f38 -e lsw-checkpoint.tar
podman container rm a89615250f38
podman container restore --keep --file-locks --tcp-established --import lsw-checkpoint.tar
```
Log:
```sh
(00.241509) pie: 2: Preadv 0x400000:4096... (15 iovs)

(00.241655)      9: 		Create fd for 118
(00.241731) pie: 6: Error (criu/pie/restorer.c:1925): Restorer fail 6
(00.241731)      9: File usr/share/java/sisu-inject.jar could only be validated with file size
(00.241787)      9: 		Create fd for 119

(00.242095) pie: 2: Task 6 exited, status= 1
(00.242117)      9: 		Create fd for 123
(00.242152)      9: File usr/share/java/plexus-cipher.jar could only be validated with file size
(00.242174)      9: 		Create fd for 124
(00.244154) mnt: Switching to new ns to clean ghosts
(00.244237) Unlink remap home/lsw/abcl-felix-cache/cache.lock.cr.1.ghost
(00.244420) Error (criu/cr-restore.c:2447): Restoring FAILED.
```

In case it matters, my podman machine is initialized with

```podman machine init --cpus 8 --memory 16384 --rootful -v /Users:/mnt/Users```
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@adrianreber @alanruttenberg shall we keep this issue open?  I am not sure how to proceed.

Looking at the last error message:
```
(00.241528) pie: 6: Error (criu/pie/restorer.c:180): can't write lsm profile -13
```

This means that the restore is happening on a system with a different LSM setup. Either no SELinux or no Apparmor. CRIU tries to restore the LSM labels and fails.

@alanruttenberg You have to make sure you restore on a system with the same LSM setup.

I have recently tested this,  successfully, in Podman 4.3.1 MacOS  10.15.7.
Thank you!
